attack_surface.exposure: SQLite shared-cache in-memory DBs are process-wide. InMemory('system') from two Engine instances connects to the SAME database.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Process boundary — only matters if multiple Engine instances exist in the same process
mitigations: InMemory() is only used when Testing.IsEnabled or Building.IsEnabled
Test runner creates fresh Engine per test and disposes between tests
gaps: No Engine.Id prefix in InMemory name — concurrent engines would silently share state
Informational only: no current code path creates parallel engines with in-memory mode

attack_surface.exposure: Sentinel SqliteConnection keeps in-memory DB alive. CRUD methods do not check _disposed flag.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Internal API — caller must manage lifecycle
mitigations: Dispose() sets _disposed flag and closes sentinel
gaps: CRUD methods (Get, Set, Remove, Exists, GetAll, Tables) do not check _disposed — use-after-dispose causes SqliteException instead of clear ObjectDisposedException

attack_surface.exposure: DeserializeValue calls Data.UnwrapJsonElement which throws InvalidOperationException on depth > 128. Only JsonException is caught in DeserializeValue. Get/GetAll only catch SqliteException | IOException.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Data stored in DataSource — could originate from external APIs via settings set
mitigations: JsonDocument.Parse default MaxDepth=64 catches JSON deeper than 64 levels as JsonException (caught)
UnwrapJsonElement's 128-depth guard is unreachable from this code path because JsonDocument.Parse fails first at 64
gaps: Violates 'behavior methods never throw' contract — if JsonDocument MaxDepth were ever raised, InvalidOperationException would escape
Defensive programming gap: the catch should anticipate all exceptions from the called method, not rely on an upstream limit

attack_surface.exposure: Table names from IDataSource.ResolveTableName (namespace-derived) and user-provided keys
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Table names from CLR type system (trusted), keys from PLang variables

attack_surface.exposure: SettingsData.GetChild intercepts dot-notation to load from DataSource on property access
attack_surface.trust_boundary: PLang variable resolution — %Settings.ApiKey% triggers DB read
mitigations: Depth parameter propagated to child.GetChild(remaining, depth + 1)
DataSource.Get returns Data (never throws) for normal operations
gaps: sync-over-async (.GetAwaiter().GetResult()) safe for SQLite but constrains future IDataSource implementations to synchronous backing stores

attack_surface.exposure: Building.IsEnabled is public { get; set; } — settable from .pr code via %!engine.Building.IsEnabled%
attack_surface.trust_boundary: User-sovereign: user owns .pr files and can set any engine property
mitigations: Actor.CreateDataSource is lazy — setting IsEnabled after DataSource is created has no effect
PLang threat model: user actions are not attacks

findings.vector: DeserializeValue catches JsonException but not InvalidOperationException from Data.UnwrapJsonElement depth guard. Get and GetAll only catch SqliteException | IOException.
findings.preconditions: Currently unreachable: JsonDocument.Parse default MaxDepth=64 rejects deep JSON before UnwrapJsonElement's 128 guard fires. Would become reachable if JsonDocumentOptions.MaxDepth were increased or if UnwrapJsonElement were called from a different path.
findings.impact: Unhandled InvalidOperationException propagates to caller, violating 'behavior methods never throw' contract. In SettingsData.GetChild path, exception would crash variable resolution.
findings.exploit_sketch: 1. Store JSON with 65+ nesting levels via settings set (requires custom JsonDocumentOptions or future API change to parse). 2. Read via settings get or %Settings.key% → DeserializeValue → UnwrapJsonElement → InvalidOperationException escapes catch blocks.
findings.proposed_fix: Add catch (InvalidOperationException ex) in DeserializeValue, return json as raw string (same pattern as the JsonException catch). Defensive: protects against future changes to parsing options.
findings.notes: Carry-forward from runtime2-system-datasource. Severity medium: currently unreachable but violates contract and is a latent bug.

findings.vector: CRUD methods (Get, Set, Remove, Exists, GetAll, Tables) do not check _disposed flag before operating. Use-after-dispose produces SqliteException (in-memory: DB gone after sentinel close) instead of clear ObjectDisposedException.
findings.preconditions: Caller must use DataSource after Actor/Engine disposal. No current code path does this.
findings.impact: Confusing error message (SqliteException vs ObjectDisposedException). No crash — exception is caught by SqliteException handlers in each method. No data corruption.
findings.exploit_sketch: N/A — internal API misuse, not an attack vector.
findings.proposed_fix: Add ThrowIfDisposed() check at the top of each CRUD method: if (_disposed) throw new ObjectDisposedException(nameof(SqliteDataSource));
findings.notes: Carry-forward from runtime2-system-datasource. Correctness issue, not security.

findings.vector: In-memory SQLite DBs with shared cache are process-wide. Two Engine instances calling InMemory('system') share state silently.
findings.preconditions: Two Engine instances in same process, both with Testing.IsEnabled or Building.IsEnabled, both creating actors with same name. No current code path does this.
findings.impact: Unexpected data sharing between engine instances. Could cause test pollution in hypothetical parallel test execution.
findings.exploit_sketch: N/A — requires parallel engine instantiation, which doesn't exist currently.
findings.proposed_fix: Use Engine.Id in the database name: InMemory($"{Engine.Id}_{Name.ToLowerInvariant()}"). Only needed if parallel engine execution is ever added.
findings.notes: By design for current usage (sequential test/build). Document the constraint.

findings.vector: sync-over-async in SettingsData.GetChild via .GetAwaiter().GetResult(). Safe for SQLite (synchronous under the hood) but would deadlock with an async IDataSource implementation.
findings.preconditions: Future IDataSource implementation with truly async operations (e.g., network-backed store) used on a SynchronizationContext-bound thread.
findings.impact: Deadlock on UI thread or ASP.NET classic. No current impact.
findings.proposed_fix: Document the constraint on IDataSource: implementations must be synchronous or use ConfigureAwait(false) internally. Or make GetChild async when the infrastructure supports it.
findings.notes: Documented in code comment. Correct for SQLite.

summary: PASS. No critical or high severity findings. One medium carry-forward (DeserializeValue InvalidOperationException gap) is currently unreachable due to JsonDocument.Parse MaxDepth=64 catching deep JSON first, but violates the 'behavior methods never throw' contract. Three low findings are correctness/design constraints, not exploitable attack vectors. The in-memory datasource feature is well-integrated with existing security infrastructure: depth guards, parameterized queries, sanitized table names, and the user-sovereign threat model all hold.
